packageText: === MASTER RADIOLOGY SOURCE PACKAGE === TITLE: Charcot (Neuropathic) / Neuropathic (Charcot) Arthropathy SOURCE PAIRING KEY: charcot_neuropathic CANONICAL HIERARCHY: Basic > Musculoskeletal > Musculoskeletal: Trauma > Introduction to Osseous Trauma > Charcot (Neuropathic) CANONICAL ANKI DECK: Corebook::MSK::Trauma::Introduction to Osseous Trauma::Charcot (Neuropathic) PRIMARY BACKBONE: RadPrimer SUPPLEMENTAL SOURCE: STATdx SOURCE DECISION: RadPrimer should drive the teaching order because its hierarchy and image progression are cleaner: location map, diabetic foot MR problem-solving, diabetic TMT foot, talonavicular progression, fluid/infection mimic, shoulder/syrinx, spine, congenital pain knee/lower limb, and rapidly destructive hip/alcoholism. STATdx should supplement with broader image recognition and staging/classification detail, especially hypertrophic diabetic Lisfranc/TMT foot, wrist, tabes/syphilis hip and knee, severe atrophic hindfoot/midfoot, shoulder mass-mimic CT/MR, Sanders-Frykberg zones, and Eichenholtz stages. STATDX COVERAGE OF RADPRIMER IMAGE SET: STATdx does not fully cover the RadPrimer image set. It contains exact or near duplicates for several RadPrimer examples, but RadPrimer has important not-covered or stronger atomic clusters: biopsy-negative fluid collection/infection mimic (RP-09..RP-10), cirrhosis etiology proof in hip Charcot (RP-22), and several cleaner same-patient timelines. Therefore all RadPrimer images remain in the primary teaching set, while duplicate STATdx images are archived. === RADPRIMER IMAGE COVERAGE GATE === - RP-01: exact duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-23; decision: Keep RadPrimer map as canonical; archive STATdx duplicate. - RP-02: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-19; decision: Keep RadPrimer atomic diabetic-foot MR cluster RP-02..RP-04; archive STATdx alternate cluster. - RP-03: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-20; decision: Keep RadPrimer same-patient cluster; archive STATdx alternate. - RP-04: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-21, SDX-22; decision: Keep RadPrimer adjacent-slice talar ON/infection problem-solving cluster intact; archive STATdx alternate cluster. - RP-05: conceptual replacement; STATdx match(es): SDX-34, SDX-35, SDX-36; decision: Keep RadPrimer radiograph/CT TMT cluster; add STATdx Lisfranc/TMT cluster as primary supplement, not replacement. - RP-06: conceptual replacement; STATdx match(es): SDX-36; decision: Keep RadPrimer CT proof with RP-05; add STATdx CT as separate supplemental diabetic-foot example. - RP-07: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-15; decision: Keep RadPrimer talonavicular early/progression pair RP-07..RP-08; archive STATdx duplicate pair. - RP-08: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-16; decision: Keep RadPrimer follow-up pair; archive STATdx duplicate pair. - RP-09: not covered; STATdx match(es): none; decision: Keep RadPrimer because it teaches neuropathic fluid collections/reactive change vs infection. - RP-10: not covered; STATdx match(es): none; decision: Keep RadPrimer with RP-09 because same-patient biopsy-negative infection mimic cluster. - RP-11: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-05; decision: Keep RadPrimer shoulder radiograph + syrinx pair; archive STATdx duplicate radiograph. - RP-12: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-06; decision: Keep RadPrimer syrinx proof; archive STATdx duplicate spine MR. - RP-13: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-11; decision: Keep RadPrimer paraplegic spine pair; archive STATdx duplicate. - RP-14: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-12; decision: Keep RadPrimer paraplegic spine pair; archive STATdx duplicate. - RP-15: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-09; decision: Keep RadPrimer congenital-insensitivity knee pair; archive STATdx duplicate. - RP-16: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-10; decision: Keep RadPrimer knee MR with same-patient radiograph; archive STATdx duplicate. - RP-17: conceptual replacement; STATdx match(es): SDX-07, SDX-08; decision: Keep RadPrimer polyarticular congenital-indifference example; add STATdx knee/ankle examples as primary supplements. - RP-18: conceptual replacement; STATdx match(es): SDX-08; decision: Keep RadPrimer contralateral limb pair; add STATdx atrophic ankle as separate primary supplement. - RP-19: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-24; decision: Keep RadPrimer hip time-lapse/alcohol-cirrhosis cluster RP-19..RP-22; archive STATdx alternate. - RP-20: exact duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-25; decision: Keep RadPrimer time-lapse cluster; archive STATdx duplicate CT. - RP-21: near duplicate; STATdx match(es): SDX-26; decision: Keep RadPrimer 7-month hip progression and hatchet sign; archive STATdx 2-month alternate to avoid timeline confusion. - RP-22: not covered; STATdx match(es): none; decision: Keep RadPrimer because cirrhosis CT proves alcoholism etiology for Charcot hip. === CURATED MASTER CONTENT === Terminology and definition: - RadPrimer: Charcot equals neuropathic joint; a severely and rapidly destructive joint process, with etiology often suggested by location. - STATdx: progressive joint destruction secondary to long-term polyneuropathy. Use this wording when teaching the mechanism, but keep RadPrimer as the canonical label. Core imaging spine: - Teach the multiple Ds / 5 Ds: normal or patient-appropriate bone density, joint distension, bony debris, cartilage or joint destruction, and joint disorganization/dislocation/deformity. STATdx also emphasizes increased density/sclerosis as part of the D pattern. - Location suggests etiology: shoulder and wrist can reflect syringomyelia; wrist and foot/ankle commonly diabetes; spine follows spinal cord injury, tabes, or diabetes, especially mobile segments caudad to a stabilized segment; hip suggests alcohol or tabes; knee suggests tabes, congenital pain indifference/insensitivity, or steroid injection; ankle/foot strongly suggests diabetes and often involves Lisfranc/TMT before talonavicular, intertarsal, Chopart, tibiotalar, or subtalar joints. - Morphology: hypertrophic form has prominent debris (classically knee); atrophic form has resorbed debris and is common in diabetic ankle/foot; combined forms are also common. - Rate of destruction can be very fast and may rival septic arthritis. Rapid hip destruction can mimic rapidly destructive osteoarthritis unless neuropathic etiology is considered. Mechanism and pathology: - Use RadPrimer as backbone: altered sympathetic control of osseous blood flow may cause hyperemia and active bone resorption; blunted pain/proprioception allows relaxation of supporting structures, chronic instability, recurrent microtrauma under abnormal loading, osseous fragmentation, and joint disorganization. - Use STATdx supplemental phrasing: polyneuropathy drives decreased pain sensation and proprioception; abnormal vascular innervation and microrepetitive trauma create osteoclast/osteoblast imbalance and osteolysis/bone resorption. - Gross/surgical feature from both sources: significant cartilaginous and osseous debris within synovial membrane. Imaging modality teaching: - Radiographs are central for diagnosis and long-term surveillance. Early disease may look normal or like osteoarthritis, with subtle subluxation, osteopenia, arch collapse in foot, or mixed cystic/erosion-like and productive changes. Middle/late disease shows debris, dislocation/subluxation, disorganization, large effusions, bone proliferation, sclerosis, and deformity. - CT is not usually required for diagnosis but is excellent for proving articular destruction, fragmentation, subluxation, and debris location; it can prevent undercalling TMT/Lisfranc destruction and can show massive debris-containing effusions that mimic mineralized masses. - MR is for early detection and problem solving. It shows marrow edema, subchondral fracture lines, effusion, soft-tissue edema, debris, intraarticular bodies, destruction, and disorganization. In the infected Charcot foot problem, T1 and postcontrast images are more useful than fluid-sensitive sequences. Confluent low T1 signal, gas/sinus tract to abnormal bone, abscess, and less debris favor superimposed osteomyelitis; hazy/reticular low T1 signal and fat replacement/reactive change favor neuropathic change. Significant overlap exists. Classification and staging from STATdx: - Sanders-Frykberg foot/ankle zones: Zone I intermetatarsal/MTP; Zone II TMT; Zone III tarsal; Zone IV ankle/subtalar; Zone V calcaneus. - Eichenholtz radiologic staging: Stage 0 normal; Stage I dislocation/fragmentation/debris with or without osteopenia; Stage II sclerosis, coalescence, resorption of fine debris, and ankylosis of large fragments; Stage III well-corticated fragments, decreased sclerosis, and chronic osseous remodeling. Differential and traps: - Infection/septic joint/osteomyelitis can coexist with Charcot. Ulcer, gas, sinus tract to abnormal bone, abscess, and confluent T1 marrow replacement support infection; disc gas at the spine favors spinal neuroarthropathy. Aspiration may be needed in difficult spine cases. - Sarcoma/chondrosarcoma trap: Charcot shoulder or other debris-containing effusions can mimic a soft-tissue mass or chondroid matrix. Charcot debris is osseous and intraarticular/effusion-based; sarcoma is typically extraarticular and matrix is not just floating articular debris. - Osteoarthritis or inflammatory/metabolic arthropathy trap: early OA can resemble early Charcot, but Charcot has larger effusions, more prominent debris, dislocation/subluxation, and much faster destruction. - Hip trap: initial radiograph may look like osteoarthritis, but rapid progression to debris, acetabular/femoral destruction, hatchet sign, and an etiology clue such as alcoholism/cirrhosis should trigger Charcot. Clinical issues and treatment: - Swelling, instability, deformity, numbness, paresthesia, and sometimes near-normal proprioception can occur; careful neurologic exam may reveal reduced deep pain/proprioception. - Diabetes is a major modern cause, especially in lower limb; RadPrimer/STATdx state 15% of diabetic patients develop Charcot joints. STATdx notes more than 10-year diabetes history is a major risk factor. Twenty percent of syringomyelia patients develop Charcot joints; 10-20% of tabes dorsalis patients develop Charcot joints worldwide. - Natural history: progressive and sometimes rapid destruction; worsening alignment increases risk of ulceration and eventual osteomyelitis. - Treatment is multidisciplinary: control/prevent infection, manage ulcers, revascularize if clinically indicated, preserve function and mobility, reconstruction/arthrodesis if conservative care fails, amputation in refractory cases. RadPrimer notes arthrodesis is difficult and recurrence/major complications occur in about 25%. === SELECTED PRIMARY IMAGE SET === - RadPrimer image 1 / RP-01 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C01-location-map Caption: Graphic shows common (red) and less common (yellow) sites of Charcot joints. Etiology is suggested by location. Use: location-etiology map; opening organizer Plain filename: RP-01_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_1.jpg Annotated filename: RP-01_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_1_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 2 / RP-02 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C02-diabetic-foot-mr-infection-fracture-on Caption: Sagittal T1WI MR, part of a series in a patient with diabetic Charcot foot, shows numerous abnormalities. Charcot joints are noted at navicular cuneiforms . There is ↓ signal in the plantar soft tissues, with confluent ↓ signal in the adjacent cuboid , concerning for osteomyelitis. It also shows diffuse ↓ signals throughout the talus , an unexpected finding. Use: Charcot foot vs osteomyelitis MR problem solving Plain filename: RP-02_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_2.jpg Annotated filename: RP-02_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_2_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 3 / RP-03 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C02-diabetic-foot-mr-infection-fracture-on Caption: Sagittal STIR MR in the same patient further defines the talar abnormality as an insufficiency fracture of the dome . Use: talar insufficiency fracture in diabetic foot Plain filename: RP-03_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_3.jpg Annotated filename: RP-03_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_3_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 4 / RP-04 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C02-diabetic-foot-mr-infection-fracture-on Caption: Sagittal T1WI C+ FS MR, adjacent slice, shows a wedge-shaped region of hypointensity , representing a segment of osteonecrosis (ON) in the body of the talus. Remember that the talus is prone to ON and that the tarsal bones are prone to insufficiency fracture in diabetic feet. Sinus track led to the cuboid, confirming osteomyelitis, which was suspected based on both T1 and STIR. Use: talar osteonecrosis and sinus tract/osteomyelitis clue Plain filename: RP-04_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_4.jpg Annotated filename: RP-04_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_4_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 5 / RP-05 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C03-diabetic-tmt-radiograph-ct Caption: AP radiograph shows swelling about the tarsometatarsal joints . Careful observation reveals fragmentation at the base of the 3rd metatarsal, with likely fragmentation of the bases of the 4th and 5th as well. There was no history of trauma; this is a Charcot foot. Use: TMT Charcot radiographic suspicion Plain filename: RP-05_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_5.jpg Annotated filename: RP-05_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_5_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 6 / RP-06 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C03-diabetic-tmt-radiograph-ct Caption: Short-axis CT, same patient, proves the fragmentation and mild subluxation of the metatarsal bases ; it is often far more severe than suspected on the basis of radiographs. As one would expect, the patient is diabetic. Use: early talonavicular Charcot Plain filename: RP-07_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_7.jpg Annotated filename: RP-07_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_7_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 8 / RP-08 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C04-talonavicular-progression Caption: Lateral radiograph of the same foot 2 months later shows ongoing destruction at the talonavicular joint . Use: 2-month progression with dorsal debris Plain filename: RP-08_RadPrimer_plain_Charcot__Neuropathic_8.jpg Annotated filename: RP-08_RadPrimer_annotated_Charcot__Neuropathic_8_annot.jpg - RadPrimer image 9 / RP-09 | primaryTeachingSet | Cluster: RP-C05-fluid-collections-biopsy-negative Caption: Sagittal T1WI C+ FS MR in a Charcot foot shows fluid collections (some containing debris) surrounding the ankle and abnormal signal within the distal tibia . Plain filename: SDX-26_STATdx_plain_Neuropathic__Charcot__Arthropathy26.jpg Annotated filename: SDX-26_STATdx_annotated_Neuropathic__Charcot__Arthropathy26_annot.jpg === IMAGE USE INSTRUCTIONS FOR FUTURE NARRATIVE/CARD GENERATION === - Always use source-qualified image labels when referencing images: e.g., RadPrimer image 5 / RP-05, STATdx image 34 / SDX-34. - If a case is a same-patient, adjacent-slice, follow-up, procedure, or etiology-proof cluster, introduce that relationship before the images are shown or tested. - If using a duplicate archived STATdx image, state why it was intentionally substituted for or added to a RadPrimer image. - Do not renumber images across sources into a single unlabeled sequence unless the extension has generated a source-qualified display registry. - For Anki cards, use the selected primary image IDs by default. Archive optional images should not be used unless the generator needs a replacement or a second example. === SOURCE ATTRIBUTION === - RadPrimer supplied the canonical article hierarchy, most same-patient teaching sequences, and the primary source facts for the 5 Ds, location-to-etiology map, Charcot foot vs osteomyelitis MR trap, debris/effusion mass mimic, and pathology mechanism. - STATdx supplied supplemental source facts for early disease, radiographic surveillance, Sanders-Frykberg and Eichenholtz classifications, additional etiologies such as amyloidosis/meningomyelocele, tabes/syphilis examples, diabetic wrist Charcot, severe hindfoot/midfoot examples, and several mass-mimic shoulder/foot cases.

imageRegistry.caption: AP radiograph shows a dislocated shoulder with severe fragmentation and bony debris. The placement of the debris suggests massive distension of the glenohumeral joint and subacromial/subdeltoid bursa . The combination of findings is typical of Charcot (neuropathic) shoulder.

imageRegistry.caption: Lateral radiograph of a paraplegic shows a burst fracture of L1 treated with partial corpectomy, strut graft , and posterior rods. imageRegistry.caption: Lateral radiograph shows significant subluxation and destruction of endplates with osseous debris at 2 adjacent levels . This paraplegic patient developed instability and Charcot spine.

imageRegistry.caption: Lateral radiograph of contralateral limb in same patient shows just as severe disruption with destruction of the tibiotalar, subtalar, and midfoot joints. Congenital indifference to pain is an unusual cause of Charcot joint and is often polyarticular.

imageRegistry.caption: AP radiograph in a middle-aged patient gives the initial impression of uncomplicated osteoarthritis, with cartilage loss , large subchondral cyst formation , superolateral subluxation of the hip, and calcar buttressing .

imageRegistry.caption: AP radiograph in the same patient 7 months later shows the classic hatchet sign of rapid destruction of the hip. imageRegistry.caption: Axial CT from the same patient shows a small, nodular liver, typical of cirrhosis. The most common etiology of Charcot hip is alcoholism; the cirrhosis proves the etiology in this case.

imageRegistry.caption: AP view of the pelvis shows destruction of the left hip , typical of a Charcot joint, in this case from tabes dorsalis. imageRegistry.caption: AP view of the hip in a patient with syphilis shows femoral shaft fracture, dislocation of the femoral head, and joint destruction with scattered debris about the joint. This is typical of a Charcot joint at the hip.

imageRegistry.caption: Lateral view shows a healed calcaneus insufficiency avulsion fracture , typical in diabetic patients. The talonavicular joint shows concavity of the articular surface of the talus, along with some sclerosis , suspicious for early Charcot joint.

imageRegistry.caption: PA view of the wrist shows neuropathic carpometacarpal joints . There is vascular calcification . This is carpal diabetes-related Charcot arthropathy.

sourceSelectionPlan.strategySummary: RadPrimer is the canonical hierarchy and narrative backbone. STATdx is used as supplemental depth where it adds stronger images, early-stage/staging detail, additional etiologies, or mass/differential pitfalls. Duplicate STATdx images are archived rather than selected.

textKeepPlan.keep: Core article spine: 5 Ds, location-to-etiology map, Charcot foot vs osteomyelitis MR problem solving, debris/effusion mass mimics, site-specific examples, pathology mechanism, treatment/prognosis.

Supplement early-stage radiographic/MR findings, Sanders-Frykberg and Eichenholtz classifications, diabetic foot surveillance language, tabes/syphilis examples, wrist example, severe atrophic foot examples, shoulder effusion/mass mimic detail.

downweightOrSkipText.skip: Near-duplicate generic 5-D/location facts already covered by RadPrimer unless wording adds specificity.
No major text exclusions; retain as canonical source.
